sections.heading: Complications
sections.content:
Hydronephrosis / Renal failure: May occur with stage IIIB disease due to pelvic sidewall extension. Management may include ureteral stenting or nephrostomy as palliative measures or to preserve renal function during treatment [17].
Fistula formation (vesicovaginal, rectovaginal): Rare at diagnosis but can be a complication of advanced disease or treatment. May require surgical repair (e.g., exenteration) or diversion procedures [18].
Lymphedema: Risk after pelvic lymphadenectomy. Managed with compression garments, manual lymphatic drainage, and exercise. Sentinel lymph node mapping may reduce risk [19,20].
Neutropenia / Febrile neutropenia: G-CSF prophylaxis may be considered, especially with regimens with high risk (>20%) of febrile neutropenia. Dose reductions or delays may be necessary [21].
Nephrotoxicity (Cisplatin): Vigorous pre- and post-hydration with normal saline is standard. Monitor renal function (BUN, creatinine). Carboplatin is an alternative for cisplatin-intolerant patients [22].
Neurotoxicity (Cisplatin, Paclitaxel): Monitor for symptoms. Dose reduction or discontinuation may be required. No standard prevention; duloxetine may offer some symptomatic relief for paclitaxel-induced neuropathy [22,23].
Emetogenic potential: High emetogenic risk with cisplatin; moderate with carboplatin. Follow NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines. Dexamethasone + 5-HT3 antagonist + NK1 receptor antagonist is typical for cisplatin [24].
Hypersensitivity reactions: Common with paclitaxel and platinum agents. For mild reactions, premedication with steroids/antihistamines. For severe reactions, the implicated agent should be avoided unless evaluated by a specialist for desensitization [25,26].
Immune-related adverse events (irAEs): Can affect any organ system. Key toxicities include colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, and dermatologic reactions. Management follows NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities (immunotherapy holds, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressants as needed) [7,27].
Bevacizumab-related hypertension, proteinuria, thrombosis, GI perforation: Monitor blood pressure and urine protein. Withhold for grade 3+ hypertension or proteinuria. Educate patients on signs/symptoms of thromboembolic events and GI perforation [28].
Tisotumab vedotin ocular toxicity (conjunctivitis, keratitis): Proactive eye care with lubricant drops, steroid eye drops, cold compresses, and wearing eye protection. Ophthalmology consultation is recommended [29,30].
Bowel, bladder, and ureteral injury: Intraoperative repair. Postoperative complications like fistulae may require delayed repair or diversion [31].
Lymphocyst, lymphedema: Lymphocyst: may resolve spontaneously or require drainage. Lymphedema: as above [19,20].
Surgical site infections, thromboembolic events: Standard perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis and VTE prophylaxis [32].
Acute: Cystitis, proctitis, diarrhea, dermatitis: Symptomatic management (antispasmodics, stool softeners, topical agents). IMRT reduces GI and GU toxicity compared to conventional RT [33,34].
Late: Fibrosis, vaginal stenosis, fistulae, bowel obstruction: Preventive: Vaginal dilator use, lubricants. Management: Surgical repair for fistulae/obstruction; hyperbaric oxygen for select cases of radiation necrosis [33,35].
Secondary malignancies: Long-term surveillance for cancers in radiation fields (e.g., colon, bladder) [36].

labels: Bevacizumab-related hypertension, proteinuria, thrombosis, GI perforation
Late: Fibrosis, vaginal stenosis, fistulae, bowel obstruction
